params.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each appear. Full separation. Worked example designation visible.
Equation builder appears showing empty template: blank × blank.

beats.text: You've been matching expressions to equal groups. Now YOU get to build them, and you'll learn to go the other way too, figuring out what an expression looks like as a picture. Let me show you how building works.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each. Full separation. Worked example.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: First, I ask myself: how many groups? Each bag is one group.

params.description: Bags highlight one by one as Guide counts.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four bags. That's 4 groups.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each. All bags highlighted after sequential count.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: Now I ask: how many in each group? Let me look inside one bag.

params.description: First bag zooms into focus, apples inside highlight.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3. Three apples. Let me check another...

elements.description: First bag in focus with 3 apples highlighted.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

params.description: Second bag highlights.

beats.text: Also 3. They're equal groups. Same number in every bag.

elements.description: Second bag highlighted showing 3 apples.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: So I have 4 groups of 3. Now watch how I build the expression.

params.description: First slot glows with subtle label "groups".

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot glowing with label "groups". Second slot empty.

beats.text: The first number is how many groups. I click 4.

params.description: 4 appears in first slot.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot filled with 4. Second slot empty.

params.description: Second slot glows with label "in each".

beats.text: The second number is how many in each group. I click 3.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot shows 4. Second slot glowing with label "in each".

params.description: 3 appears in second slot. Complete expression shows: 4 × 3.

beats.text: I built 4 times 3. This is called an expression. An expression uses numbers and symbols to show the math. No equal sign. Just the numbers and the times symbol.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
Complete expression: 4 × 3.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6 × 3: reversed the numbers

params.description: Bags highlight one by one.

steps.text: Count the bags first. How many bags do you see? That number goes in the first slot.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 + 6: used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: Equal groups structure highlights: bags and items within.

steps.text: These are equal groups. We use multiplication, not addition. Look for the times symbol between the numbers.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6 × 6: counted items instead of recognizing group structure

params.description: First bag highlights to show it is one group.

steps.text: Each bag is one group. Count how many bags there are. Then count how many items are in one bag.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially as Guide counts.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the bags: 1, 2, 3. Three bags. That is 3 groups, so the first number is 3. Now I look inside one bag and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six items in each bag. That is 6 in each group, so the second number is 6. The expression is 3 times 6.

params.description: Left side: 2 bags, 5 items in each bag. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.
Right side: 2 boxes, 5 items in each box. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.

beats.text: Look at these two. On the left, bags. On the right, boxes.

Right side: 2 boxes, 5 items in each box. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.

Boxes highlight.

beats.text: Different containers. But notice what's the SAME.

Right side: 2 boxes highlighted, 5 items in each box. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.

Items in one box highlight.

beats.text: Different containers, bags or boxes, but the SAME structure. Both are 2 groups of 5. Both build the same expression: 2 times 5.

elements.description: Left side: 2 bags, 5 items in each. Expression 2 × 5 highlighted.
Right side: 2 boxes, 5 items in each. Expression 2 × 5 highlighted.

beats.text: Equal groups work the same way no matter what holds them.

elements.description: Left side: 2 bags, 5 items in each. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.
Right side: 2 boxes, 5 items in each. Expression 2 × 5 shown below.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers but not all (either A or C, not both)

params.description: Option A highlights.
Option C highlights.

steps.text: You found one that works. Look at all the bags again. Is there another one where every bag has the same number?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected no correct answers (only B, only D, both B and D, or neither)

params.description: Option A highlights.

steps.text: Equal groups means every bag has the SAME amount. Look at the first picture. Count the items in each bag. Are they all the same?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect

params.description: Option B highlights.
Option D highlights.

steps.text: Some of your choices work, but check these highlighted ones. Count the items in each bag. Do they all have the same number?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A bags highlight sequentially showing 4, 4, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. The first picture has 3 bags. Count the items: 4 in the first bag, 4 in the second, 4 in the third. All the same, so equal groups.

params.description: Option C bags highlight sequentially showing 3, 3, 3, 3.

steps.text: The third picture has 4 bags: 3 in each one. All the same, equal groups. The correct answers are A and C because every bag in those pictures has the same number inside.

params.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each appear on screen.
Equation builder appears with two blank slots and multiplication symbol. Tile palette shows tiles 1 through 10.

beats.text: You've learned that you need equal groups to write multiplication expressions and that equal groups work the same way no matter what holds them. These are important ideas for multiplication.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder with two blank slots and tile palette showing tiles 1 through 10.

beats.text: You've been choosing complete expressions to represent equal groups. Now you'll build them piece by piece using number tiles. Watch.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder with two blank slots and tile palette.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially.

beats.text: Notice all of the groups are equal. Same questions: How many groups?
5 boxes. That's 5 groups. I need the 5 tile in the first slot.

params.description: Guide demonstrates picking up 5 tile and placing it in first slot.
Tile 5 snaps into first slot. Expression now shows 5 × __.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder showing 5 × __ with second slot still blank.

params.description: Items in one box highlight.

beats.text: How many in each group?
2 items. I need the 2 tile in the second slot.

params.description: Guide demonstrates placing 2 tile in second slot.
Tile 2 snaps into second slot. Complete expression now shows 5 × 2.

beats.text: I built it piece by piece. 5 times 2. Now you try with tiles.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder showing complete expression 5 × 2.

incorrects.description: Student reversed the numbers: placed 4 × 2 instead of 2 × 4

params.description: Bags highlight to draw attention to group count.

steps.text: You switched the numbers. Count the bags first. That's your groups number. Then count inside one bag. That's your in-each number.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt, not the reversed case

steps.text: Let's look closely. How many bags do you see?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt, not the reversed case

params.description: Bags highlight one by one as system counts.

steps.text: Count the bags: 1, 2. That's 2 groups. Now pick one bag and count what's inside. How many items?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight one by one. Then items in one bag highlight.
System places 2 tile in first slot, then 4 tile in second slot.
System places 4 tile in second slot.

steps.text: Here's how to build it. First I count the bags. 1, 2. Two bags means 2 groups, so I place 2 in the first slot. Then I look inside one bag and count the items. 1, 2, 3, 4. Four items means 4 in each group, so I place 4 in the second slot. The answer is 2 times 4, because that's what the picture shows: 2 groups of 4.

incorrects.description: Student reversed the numbers, placing 5 × 3 instead of 3 × 5

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one to emphasize counting groups.

steps.text: You placed 5 times 3, but look at the groups. Count the boxes: 1, 2, 3. That's 3 groups. Now count inside one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. That's 5 in each group. What expression shows 3 groups of 5?

incorrects.description: Student placed an incorrect expression on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the boxes first. That's how many groups you have.

incorrects.description: Student placed an incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one as system counts.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the boxes: 1, 2, 3. The first number is how many groups, so that's 3. Now look inside one box. Count those items. How many do you see in each?

incorrects.description: Student placed an incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially as system counts.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count the groups. That's the boxes: 1, 2, 3. Three groups. That number goes first, so I place the 3 tile.

params.description: System places 3 tile in first slot.

steps.text: Next, I count how many in each group. I look inside one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five items. That number goes second, so I place the 5 tile.

params.description: Items in one box highlight as system counts them.
System places 5 tile in second slot, completing 3 × 5.

steps.text: The expression is 3 times 5. That's what the picture shows: 3 groups of 5. The first number always tells you how many groups, and the second number tells you how many in each group.

params.description: Equation Builder in expression mode displays complete expression 2 × 6. No slots, read-only.
Option A: 2 bags with 6 items each.
Option B: 6 bags with 2 items each.
Option C: 3 bags with 4 items each.

beats.text: We've been going from pictures to expressions. Let's flip it around. If I see 2 times 6, what does that MEAN?

elements.description: Equation Builder displays 2 × 6 in expression mode.
Option A: 2 bags with 6 items each.
Option B: 6 bags with 2 items each.
Option C: 3 bags with 4 items each.

params.description: Option A bags flash subtly.

beats.text: The first number is groups. So 2 groups.

Option B: 6 bags with 2 items each.
Option C: 3 bags with 4 items each.

params.description: Items inside Option A bags highlight.

beats.text: The second number is in each group. So 6 in each.

Option C: 3 bags with 4 items each.

params.description: Option A highlights completely.

beats.text: The expression tells you what to look for. 2 times 6 MEANS 2 groups with 6 in each.

steps.text: The first number tells you how many groups. Look at the expression. How many groups do you need?

incorrects.description: Student selected option C: scattered items with no groups

params.description: The expression 5 × 3 highlights.

steps.text: Multiplication shows equal groups. Look for a picture with groups, not scattered items. How many groups do you see in the expression?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A bags highlight one by one as system counts.

steps.text: Let me show you. The expression says 5 times 3. The first number, 5, tells us how many groups. Count the bags in Option A: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five groups. The second number, 3, tells us how many in each group. Look inside any bag: 3 items. Option A matches because it shows 5 groups with 3 in each. That is what 5 times 3 means.

incorrects.description: Student set container count to incorrect value on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the first number in the expression. How many groups?

incorrects.description: Student set container count to incorrect value on second attempt

params.description: First number 4 highlights in the expression.

steps.text: The first number tells you how many groups. 4 times 5 means 4 groups. Set the container count to 4.

incorrects.description: Student set container count to incorrect value three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting container count to 4. Four empty containers appear.

steps.text: Let me show you. The first number in a multiplication expression is the number of groups. 4 times 5 means 4 groups. I set the container count to 4, and now we have 4 empty containers. That matches the first number in the expression.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value on first attempt

steps.text: Let's take another look. The second number tells you how many in each group.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value on second attempt

params.description: Second number 5 highlights in the expression.

steps.text: The second number tells you how many in each group. 4 times 5 means 5 in each. Set the items per container to 5.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting items per container to 5. Five items populate each of the four containers.

steps.text: Here's how it works. The second number in a multiplication expression is how many in each group. 4 times 5 means 5 in each group. I set items per container to 5. Now each of the 4 containers has exactly 5 items. That's what 4 times 5 means: 4 groups with 5 in each.

steps.text: Look at the circles. How many circles are there? How many dots in each circle?

steps.text: The first number is how many groups. Count the circles. The second number is how many in each group. Count the dots inside one circle. Place those two numbers.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Circles highlight one by one as Guide counts.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, I count the groups. That means count the circles: 1, 2. Two circles, so the first number is 2.

params.description: Dots in one circle highlight.

steps.text: Now I count how many in each group. I look inside one circle: 1, 2, 3. Three dots, so the second number is 3. The expression is 2 times 3. That's what these circles show, 2 groups of 3.

steps.text: Look at the circles. How many groups do you see?

steps.text: Count the circles. That's your first number. Then count the dots in ONE circle. That's your second number. Place those tiles.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four circles, so 4 groups.

params.description: Dots in one circle highlight.

steps.text: Now I look inside one circle: 1, 2, 3, 4 dots. Four in each group. So the expression is 4 times 4. The first 4 tells me how many groups. The second 4 tells me how many in each group.

params.description: First number 5 in expression highlights.

steps.text: The first number tells you how many groups. Look at 5 times 6. How many groups does that mean?

incorrects.description: Student selected Option C: scattered dots without containers, no grouping structure

steps.text: Multiplication shows equal groups. You need to see groups, not scattered items. Which picture shows groups?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Numbers in expression highlight sequentially: first 5, then 6.
Option A demonstrates counting: circles pulse one by one showing 5 groups, then items in one circle highlight showing 6 in each.

steps.text: Here's how to read 5 times 6. The first number is groups. I count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five groups. The second number is in each group. I count inside one circle: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six in each. So 5 times 6 means 5 groups with 6 in each group. That's Option A.
